steps.title: Identify what the hypothesis claims
steps.content:
The researchers hypothesize that the plants depend on acid-dissolving rock for two reasons: (1) it creates growth channels and (2) it releases phosphates for nutrition. To support this, a finding must show that the plants actively dissolve rock even when there is an easier alternative — proving the behavior is purposeful, not just opportunistic. Eliminate choices that are irrelevant to the mechanism or that contradict the hypothesis.
  • Choice A — 'Other species in the Velloziaceae family are found in terrains with more soil but have root structures similar to those of B. tomentosa and B. macrantha.': Relates to other species' anatomy in different terrain. Does not show whether the two studied plants dissolve rock on purpose. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Though B. tomentosa and B. macrantha both secrete citric and malic acids, each species produces the acids in different proportions.': Notes a difference between the two species' acid ratios. Does not address whether dissolving rock is necessary or advantageous for either. ✗
  • Choice C — 'The roots of B. tomentosa and B. macrantha carve new entry points into rocks even when cracks in the surface are readily available.': Shows the plants bypass the easy path and dissolve rock anyway — directly implying they gain something (e.g., phosphates) that makes the effort worthwhile. This supports the hypothesis. ✓
  • Choice D — 'B. tomentosa and B. macrantha thrive even when transferred to the surfaces of rocks that do not contain phosphates.': Contradicts the hypothesis: if the plants thrive without phosphates, releasing phosphates through acid-dissolving may not be the critical benefit. ✗

steps.title: Match the finding to the hypothesis
steps.content: The only finding that shows the plants choose to dissolve rock over a simpler alternative is C. That choice directly supports the hypothesis that acid-dissolving is purposeful and necessary. That matches choice C — C is correct.
